question: How much does apple make on ipads
answer: Based on the financial report, Apple made **$8,595 million** from iPad sales for the three months ended December 27, 2025. This is how much money they brought in from selling iPads during that period.

question: What is apple's total income
answer:
Apple's total income, also known as Net income, for the three months ended December 27, 2025, was $42,097 million. This is the company's profit after all expenses, including taxes, have been paid.

For comparison, for the three months ended December 28, 2024, Apple's Net income was $36,330 million.

question: Is apple making money
answer:
I couldn't find that in the document.

The document provided is a "CONDENSED CONSOLIDATED BALANCE SHEETS." A balance sheet shows what a company owns (assets), what it owes (liabilities), and the owners' stake (shareholders' equity) at a specific point in time. It doesn't tell you how much money the company made or lost over a period, which is typically found on an income statement.

question: Is apple making money
answer:
Yes, Apple is making money.

We can see this in the "CONDENSED CONSOLIDATED STATEMENTS OF OPERATIONS" under "Net income."

*   For the three months ended December 27, 2025, Apple had a net income of **$42,097 million**.
*   For the three months ended December 28, 2024, Apple had a net income of **$36,330 million**.

"Net income" is the company's profit after all expenses and taxes have been paid. Since these numbers are positive, it means Apple has more money coming in than going out, so they are making a profit.

question: Define heterotrait heteromethod correlations
answer: Heterotrait-heteromethod correlations refer to when you are measuring different things (constructs) using different ways (methods).
chunks_retrieved: Heterotrait-heteromethod correlations 71 Monotrait-monomethod correlation Same constructs measured using the same method Monotrait-heteromethod correlations Same constructs measured using different methods Heterotrait-monomethod correlations Different constructs measured using the same method Heterotrait-heteromethod correlations Different constructs measured using different methods Week 5 • Descriptive statistics describe your dataset (figures, sum- mary stats) • Probability theory makes a prediction (which events should I expect) • Inferential statistics draw a conclusion (I know what data are and what they came to be what should i conclude) • Frequentists vs Bayesian: Frequentists probability is long run frequency EX if you flip a perfectly fair coin a LOT of times, about half will be heads. In Bayesian, probability is degree of belief, subjective and is belief of rational agents EX: I have 50% degree of belief that the coin is heads • R example functionss include dbinom (probab density/pmf of specific outcome), pbinom (CDF ¡= x), qbinom (inverse CDF„ given prob find x), rbinom (sample random number from a distribution) • Estimate is best guess of true population parameter based on sample of data. Good estimators are unbiassed, consis- tent, have low variance • Estimator of µ is ¯ X or M • Estimator of σ is s = q 1 N PN i=1 (Xi −¯ X2) where ˆσ is same thing but N-1 denom due to unbiassed • Sampling distribution of mean uses SEM instead of SD SEM = σ √ N (as sample size grows uncertainty about mean goes down) • CLT states sampling distribution of mean no matter under- lying distribution becomes normal • CI95 = ¯ X ± 1.96 ˆσ √ N (range that covers the mean 95% of the time) • Study has two different issues, is study valid (research method), and does data suggest there is real effect (statis- tics) • NHST asks how likely were we to have seen the data we did if the null hypothesis were true • Research hypotheses are claims about psychological con- structs (IE people agree with one another about whether we are running out of food), statistical hypotheses are claimss about population paramters (IE θ ̸= 0.5 • Type I error: Reject H0 given H0 true (α) (NHST controls this) • Type II error: Do not reject H0 given H0 false (β) and (1 −β) is power IE reject H0 given H0 false. Note power depends on sample size, effect size and α. All else equal, lower α means higher β • Everything else constant, sample size doesn’t impact type I and reduces type II. Everything else constant, decreasing expected type I, increases expected type II and vice versa • To build statistical test, need (1) Diagnostic test statistic T (eg mean), (2) Sampling distribution of T if null is true (3) The observed T in your data (4) A rule that maps every value of T onto decision (accept or reject H0 • Neyman says p-value describes type I error rate willing to tolerate if want to reject H0, fisher says probability given H0
